coredata.dc:title: A red-emitting Europium(III) complex as a luminescent probe with large Stokes shift for the sequential determination of Cu2+ and biothiols in real samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jie
Zhou, Xibin
Wang, Jun
Fang, Dawei
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) complex with red luminescence was designed and synthesized for sequential detection of Cu2+ and biothiols (Cys/Hcy/GSH) based on the displacement strategy with the good selectivity, high sensitivity, and large Stokes shift (288 nm). The possible detection mechanism was verified by UV–vis, the high-resolution mass spectrometry, and the fluorescence decay curve. The experimental parameters, including the solution pH, the incubation time, the concentration ratio of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) to Cu2+ and biothiols concentration, were optimized. Under the optimal conditions, it shows a good linear relationship between the concentration (0–10 μM) of Cu2+ and the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC), with a low detection limit of 0.065 μM. The linear range and the limit of detection of the Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ system for Cys/Hcy/GSH were 2.5–22.5/5–45/5–50 μM and 0.11/0.07/0.05 μM, respectively. Surprisingly, the high or low concentration of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ can significantly affect the selectivity of the sensing system to biothiols (Cys/GSH/Hcy). When the concentration of the Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ system is 10.0 μΜ, it could recognize biothiols (Cys/GSH/Hcy) from other substances, but when the concentration is as low as 3.3 μM, it could further specifically distinguished Cys from Hcy/GSH. Owing to the high anti-interference characteristics, accuracy and specificity, the sensing system was well applied to the cascade detection of Cu2+ in actual environmental samples and Cys in biological and food samples, including FBS, urine, milk, beverage, fresh juice with the satisfactory recoveries from 96.20 to 106.80 %.

Fluorescence response (a) of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) (10 μM) toward various ions (15 μM) with (red bars) and without (blue bars) Cu2+ (15 μM). The fluorescence emission spectra (b) of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) (10 μM) in the presence of Cu2+ (0–100 μM). The changes in the fluorescence intensity (c) of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) (10 μM) vs Cu2+ concentration (0–100 μM). Inset: the linear relationship of F0/F vs Cu2+ concentration (0–10 μM). Experimental conditions: TEAA buffer solution (50 mM, pH 4.5), λex/em = 330/618 nm. T = 30 min.

The relationship (a) between F0/F and concentration of Cu2+ from 0 to 100 μM at 618 nm. Fluorescence decay curves (b) of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) (10 μM) before and after the addition of Cu2+ (10 μM). λex/em = 330/618 nm.

The fluorescence emission spectra (a) and the photographs (a) of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) (10 μM), Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) (10 μM) + Cu2+ (10 μM) and Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) (10 μM) + Cu2+ (10 μM) + Cys/Hcy/GSH (50 μM), the fluorescence recovery efficiency (b) of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ (10 μM) at 618 nm upon addition of various amino acids (50 μM). Inset: the relationship between the coresponding emission intensity at 618 nm and the concentration of Cys/Hcy/GSH, λex/em = 330/618 nm.

The HRMS spectra (a) of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) and following the addition of Cu2+ and biothiols (Cys/Hcy/GSH) in DMSO‑d 6; the UV–vis spectra (b) of Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC) (10 μM), Cu2+ containing samples (Cys/Hcy/GSH) and Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) (10 μM) sequentially in the addition of Cu2+ (10 μM) and biothiols (Cys/ Hcy/GSH) (50 μM) in TEAA buffer solution (50 mM, pH 4.5).

head.ce:title: A red-emitting Europium(III) complex as a luminescent probe with large Stokes shift for the sequential determination of Cu2+ and biothiols in real samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) complex with red luminescence was designed and synthesized for sequential detection of Cu2+ and biothiols (Cys/Hcy/GSH) based on the displacement strategy with the good selectivity, high sensitivity, and large Stokes shift (288 nm). The possible detection mechanism was verified by UV–vis, the high-resolution mass spectrometry, and the fluorescence decay curve. The experimental parameters, including the solution pH, the incubation time, the concentration ratio of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) to Cu2+ and biothiols concentration, were optimized. Under the optimal conditions, it shows a good linear relationship between the concentration (0–10 μM) of Cu2+ and the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC), with a low detection limit of 0.065 μM. The linear range and the limit of detection of the Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ system for Cys/Hcy/GSH were 2.5–22.5/5–45/5–50 μM and 0.11/0.07/0.05 μM, respectively. Surprisingly, the high or low concentration of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ can significantly affect the selectivity of the sensing system to biothiols (Cys/GSH/Hcy). When the concentration of the Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ system is 10.0 μΜ, it could recognize biothiols (Cys/GSH/Hcy) from other substances, but when the concentration is as low as 3.3 μM, it could further specifically distinguished Cys from Hcy/GSH. Owing to the high anti-interference characteristics, accuracy and specificity, the sensing system was well applied to the cascade detection of Cu2+ in actual environmental samples and Cys in biological and food samples, including FBS, urine, milk, beverage, fresh juice with the satisfactory recoveries from 96.20 to 106.80 %.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the investigations of metal ions and small molecular biothiols have attracted great attention because of their important role in maintaining redox homeostasis and electron transfer processes in organisms [1–3]. Copper ion is not only the third abundant trace metal element in the human body (except zinc and iron) [4], but also an important metal pollutant of the natural environment due to its widespread used in industry. It has been reported that the abnormal concentrations of Cu2+ in the body are related to various diseases, such as neurodegenerative diseases [5], gastrointestinal disturbance, haematological manifestations, and damage of the liver and kidneys [1,6]. In the environment, the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the World Health Organization (WHO) set the maximum concentration of Cu2+ in drinking water to be 1.3 ppm (20 μM) and 31.5 μM [7,8], respectively. On the other hand, biothiols, including cysteine (Cys) and homocysteine (Hcy), glutathione (GSH), play critical roles in physiological and pathological processes in biological systems [9–11]. The deficiency or elevated levels of these biothiols are associated with various human diseases, such as growth retardation, liver injury, Alzheimer's, osteoporosis, cancer, cardiovascular disease [12,13]. At the same time, Cys plays an important role in the pharmaceutical, cosmetics and food industries [14]. For example, it is an essential component in the formula of some drugs and skin care products. It is often used as a flavoring agent in the food industry. In view of the above reasons, it is particularly urgent to develop the efficient strategy for sensitive and accurate identification and quantitative analysis of Cu2+ and biothiols in real samples.

At present, many analytical approaches for detecting Cu2+ and biothiols have been established, such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [15,16], capillary electrophoresis [17,18], voltammetry [19,20], and high-performance liquid chromatography [21,22], etc. Nevertheless, these methods often require more harsh conditions, such as expensive equipment, time-consuming, and complicated operations. The fluorescence assay, a powerful optical technology, has attracted more and more attention because of their simple operation, in-situ real-time detection, high sensitivity, good selectivity, non-invasive, and high spatial resolution. Hitherto, many fluorescent probes have been developed to distinguish biothiols from other amino acids and applied to biological imaging. They are mainly designed based on the following reactions: cleavage of sulfonamide/sulfonate ester [23], breaking of ether/ester bond [24,25], cleavage of disulfide [26,27], Michael addition [28,29], and cyclization with aldehyds [30,31]. But, these fluorescent probes were rarely used for the determination of some analytes in actual samples in the fields of environment, biology and food. Moreover, lanthanide complexes and materials have unique photophysical properties, including good solubility, sharply-spiked emission spectra (10 nm bandwidth), large Stokes shift (no overlap in excitation and emission spectra), and long luminescence lifetime (micro to millisecond scale) [32–34]. These advantages make them an ideal candidate for the detection of small molecules in real samples [35–38].

Herein, a novel lanthanide chelate Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) with large Stokes shift was designed and synthesized to detect Cu2+ and biothiols sequentially in real samples by the “on-off-on” fluorescence type using the displacement assays. This “on-off-on” fluorescent probe could overcome the disadvantages of poor repeatability and false-positive signal of single off/on mode, reduce the impact on other environments [39], and effectively improve the accuracy and effectiveness of the detection system. To date, the detection of Cu2+ by some lanthanide complexes was mostly based on paramagnetism [40], electron transfer quenching [7] or energy transfer [41]. However, the detection of Cu2+ and biothiols by lanthanide fluorescent probes based on the substitution reaction has scarcely been reported [42]. Surprisingly, it was first found that the high or low concentration of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ system had a particular relationship with the specific selection of biothiols (Cys/Hcy/GSH). When the concentration of the Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ system is 10.0 μΜ, it could recognize biothiols (Cys/Hcy/GSH) from other substances, but when the concentration is 3.3 μM, it could further specifically distinguished Cys from Hcy/GSH. More importantly, the developed method could be successfully applied to determine of copper ions in environmental water samples and Cys in fetal bovine serum (FBS), urine, milk, beverage and juice with the satisfactory results.

All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and can be purchased commercially. No further purification was performed before use. Diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA), europium nitrate hexahydrate (EuNO3·6H2O), and 7-amino-4-methyl coumarin (AMC) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., ltd. (Shanghai), copper sulfate pentahydrate, other inorganic salts, and various amino acids were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. The water used throughout the experiments was ultrapure (a resistivity of 18.3 MΩ·cm−1). Triethylammonium acetate (TEAA) buffer solution (50 mM) of the different pH values were prepared from anhydrous acetic acid and triethylamine in different proportions to the required pH values. Lake water samples were collected in Jinling reservoir (located in Jinzhou, Liaoning Province, China).

The absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of all samples were measured with an ultraviolet spectrometer (Cary 50, Varian company, USA) and fluorophotometer (Cary 300, Varian company, USA), respectively. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra of DTPA-bis(AMC) were recorded with nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrometer (technologies plus-400 Mr, Agilent company, USA), and the solvent and internal standard were DMSO-D6 and tetramethylsilane (TMS), respectively. The high-resolution mass spectra were collected by Fourier transform ion cyclotron resonance mass spectrometer FT-ICR MS (apex ultra Bruker, USA). The fluorescence decay times were determined by the full-function steady/transient state fluorescence spectrometer of Edinburgh instruments (FLS1000, UK) with the pulse duration of 250 Hz and time resolution of 1 μs under 330 nm excitation. Fluorescence lifetime was assessed with the Edinburgh built-in analysis software. MP 220 pH meter (Mettler Toledo, Switzerland) was used to measure pH values of the solutions.

According to the previous work of our laboratory [43], the diethylenetriamine pentaacetic acid dianhydride (DTPAA) was synthesized, as shown in Scheme 1 . Diethylenetriamine-pentaacetic acid (DTPA) (7.8 g, 20 mmol) was added to a stirred solution of acetic anhydride (8.00 mL, 85 mmol) and pyridine (20 mL, 240 mmol) under anhydrous condition. Then the mixed solution was heated at 70 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, it was cooled to room temperature and the solvent was removed by vacuum distillation. The crude product was washed three times with acetic anhydride and followed by anhydrous ether. Finally, the product was placed in a vacuum drying oven and dried at 80 °C for 3 h to obtain 5.55 g beige powder. The yield was 74 %. The DTPAA was not further purified and was used directly in the following reaction.

The synthesis of DTPA-bis(AMC) is slightly modified according to the previous literature [32,44]. The synthesis process was shown in Scheme. 0.125 g (0.33 mmol) DTPAA, 0.13 g (0.74 mmol) AMC and 100 μL dry triethylamine were added to 10 mL dry DMF. The reaction was stirred at room temperature for 12 h. Finally, the product was dried under high vacuum (52 kPa) at 50 °C to obtain a light yellow solid. The yield is about 54 %. 1H NMR (Fig. S1) (400 MHz, d6-DMSO) δ 2.319(s, 6H), 2.990(s, 4H), 3.093(s, 4H), 3.484(s, 4H), 3.522(s, 4H), 3.587(s, 2H), 6.181(s, 2H), 7.525(s, 4H), 7.734 (s, 2H), 10.474 (s, 3H). 13C NMR (Fig. S2) (101 MHz, d6-DMSO) δ 18.4, 51.3, 52.8, 55.5, 55.9, 58.8, 106.2, 112.6, 115.3, 115.8, 125.9, 142.5, 153.4, 153.9, 160.4, 169.9, 170.6, 173.5. HRMS (Fig. 8a) m/z calcd. for C34H36N5O12 − [M−H]− 706.23660, found 706.23570.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of various solutions and measurements of spectra
ce:section.ce:para: Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) was synthesized by self-assembly of DTPA-bis(AMC) and Eu(NO3)3 in TEAA buffer solution (50 mM, pH 4.5), as shown in Scheme 1. 20 μL of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O in water (1 × 10−3 M) and 20 μL of DTPA-bis(AMC) in water (1 × 10−3 M) were added to 1.96 mL of TEAA buffer solution (50 mM, pH 4.5). After mixing evenly, it was still at room temperature for 5 min as Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) solution (1 × 10−5 M). HRMS (Fig. 8a) m/z calcd. for C34H34EuN5O12 − [M−H]− 856.13436, found 856.13435.

Various metal ions (1 × 10−3 M) (LiCl, NaCl, MgCl2, KCl, AlCl3, CaCl2, Zn(OAC)2, CuCl2, NiCl2, Pb(NO3)2, MnCl2, FeCl2, AgNO3, CoCl2, Cd(NO3)2 and FeCl3) and amino acids (1 × 10−3 M) (Cysteine (Cys), dl-homocysteine (Hcy), Glutathione (GSH), Glutamic (Glu), Arginine (Arg), Aspartic acid (Asp), Glycine (Gly), Histidine (His), Lysine (Lys), Phenylalanine (Phe), Serine (Ser), Threonine (Thr), Tryptophan (Trp), Tyrosine (Tyr), and Valine (Val)) were dissolved in the ultrapure water.

For the detection of Cu2+, 20 μL of Eu3+ (1 × 10−3 M) and 20 μL of DTPA-bis(AMC) (1 × 10−3 M) were added to TEAA buffer solution, and after 10 min an appropriate volume of Cu2+ was added to the above solution, and ensure that the total volume is 2 mL. The fluorescence spectra and UV–vis Spectra were measured after 30 min. Then, in order to determine the selectivity of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) to Cu2+, under the same experimental conditions, the fluorescence intensity was measured after various metal ions substituted for Cu2+. For the detection of Cys/Hcy/GSH, an appropriate amount of Cys/Hcy/GSH was added to Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ (10 μM) in TEAA buffer solution (50 mM and pH 4.5). The fluorescence intensities and UV–vis signals were recorded after a specific incubation time. In addition, the selectivity of biothiols in the system of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ was carried out in the same process by replacing biothiols with main amino acids. The excitation and emission slit widths were set at 10 nm. All experiments were carried out at room temperature.

In order to explore the possibility of applicability of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) system in practical samples, Cu2+ in actual water samples and biothiols in FBS, urine, milk, beverage and fresh juice were detected by the standard addition method [6,45,46], respectively. The contents of biological thiols in the actual samples were determined with Cys as the standard [47].

All experiments were carried out in accordance with the corresponding laws and institutional guidelines. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Zhejiang Tianhang Biotechnology Co., ltd. The urine samples were collected from three healthy volunteers with the consent of the ethics committee of Jinzhou Medical University. Pure milk (240 mL/bag, pasteurized and full fat milk), flavored beverage (500 mL) and fresh apples were purchased from a local supermarket and used within the shelf-life. The pretreatment of actual samples can be seen in the ESM [47–49]. The corresponding contents of Cu2+ and Cys were calculated according to the standard curve obtained in this work. The samples of each concentration were measured three times.

Lanthanide chelates are generally composed of lanthanide ions, polycarboxylic acid chelators, and organic fluorophores. In this work, DTPA-bis(AMC), as a symmetrical ligand, is complexed with Eu3+ to form a fluorescent complex Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC). The organic fluorophore 7-amino-4-methyl coumarin (AMC) acts as an antenna to transfer the absorbed energy to Eu3+, which can sensitize Eu3+, that is, the single excited state S1 of AMC fluorophore is transferred to the group state of lanthanide metal ion Eu3+ through antenna fluorophore triplet state T1 (5D0), accompanied by the inactivation of 7F J ground states (J = 0–4) when excited at 330 nm [50]. As can be seen from Fig. 1 a, there is an absorption band centered at 325.1 nm in the DTPA-bis(AMC) solution, which is the characteristic absorption peak of AMC [51]. When Eu3+ is added, the intensity of the absorption peak at 325.1 nm decreases with concomitant appearance by blue shift to 321.0 nm. At the same time, a small peak appears at 292.8 nm, indicating that Eu3+ and DTPA-bis(AMC) undergo complexation reaction, and AMC transfers the absorbed energy to Eu3+, which is consistent with that reported in the literature [44,51], further convincingly confirming the formation of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) with Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: After the addition of Cu2+ to the solution, the strong red fluorescence of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) was quenched largely, which was attributed to the fact that Cu2+ could replace Eu3+ in TEAA buffer solution (50 mM, pH 4.5) to form a non-fluorescent complex Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC).

Before the quantitative determination of Cu2+, the optimal experiments were carried out to obtain better sensitivity. Firstly, the effect of the acidity of the TEAA buffer solution on the quenching efficiency (F/F0) was investigated. The greater the quenching efficiency, and thus the higher the detection sensitivity [56]. As shown in Fig. S3, the pH of the solution has a certain relationship with the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) and the quenching efficiency of Cu2+ on Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC). As can be seen from Fig. S3b when the acidity of the solution increases from 4 to 9, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) first increases and then decreases. A higher fluorescence intensity can be reached in the pH range of 4.5–6. When the same concentration of Cu2+ was added to Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) system with different pH values, the fluorescence quenching efficiency showed great differences. When pH > 7, Cu2+ partially hydrolyzed and then chelated with OH− to form Cu(OH)n 2−
n complex [45], which seriously affects the substitution of Cu2+ for Eu3+, resulting in low quenching efficiency. When the pH of the solution is less than 4.5, the acidity of the solution is too strong, resulting in the protonation of carboxylate, which significantly affects the coordination between carboxylate and europium ion. Therefore, pH 4.5 is the best solution acidity for Cu2+ substitute the Eu3+ in the complex. This conclusion is consistent with that reported in the literature [57,58]. In other words, at pH 4.5, the complexation constant of Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC) is larger than that of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC). The pH plays a key catalytic role in the displacing reaction [Eq. (1)] [59].

The quenching efficiency is also closely correlated with the incubation time. Overall, the substitution reaction can reach a dynamic equilibrium at about 30 min. Therefore, the incubation time of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) with Cu2+ is selected as 30 min for following up experiments.

Specificity is an important index of the excellent fluorescent probe. Selective and competitive experiments were carried out to confirm the specificity of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) system for Cu2+. Under the same experimental conditions, 24 ions (Cu2+, Ag+, Li+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni+, Ba2+, Al3+, Cr3+, F−, Br−, CNS−, NO2 −, IO4 −, ClO−, H2PO4 +, HPO4 2−, P2O7 2+) were added respectively to the Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) system to record their fluorescence intensities at 618 nm. As depicted in the blue columns in Fig. 2 a, the addition of other ions did not cause clear fluorescent quenching of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) at 618 nm, except for Pb2+ (F/F0 = 37.5 %) and Fe3+ (F/F0 = 35.1 %), but their quenching efficiency was significantly lower than that of Cu2+ (as high as 78.2 %). Competitive experiments were conducted to determine whether these ions could interfere with the detection of Cu2+. When Cu2+ was added to the Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) system containing various ions, the fluorescence intensity of the system decreased significantly (in the red columns in Fig. 2a). These results show that the detection of Cu2+ by Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) is specific and free from the interference of other ions.

In order to understand the fluorescence response of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) to Cu2+, fluorescence titration experiments were carried out. As described in Fig. 2b-c, the fluorescence intensities at 618 nm of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) decreased gradually with the increase of Cu2+ concentration (0–100 μM), and finally reached a dynamic equilibrium. There was a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9936) between the fluorescence intensities at 618 nm of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) and the concentrations of Cu2+ ranging from 0 to 10 μM. The linear equation is: y = −95.25926x + 1473.17704. The limit of detection is calculated as 0.065 μM based on LOD = 3σ/k. This obtained LOD of Cu2+ is far lower than the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) (20 μM) and WHO (30 μM) safety limit of copper ion in drinking water. It indicates that Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) as a probe is highly sensitive and selective to Cu2+.

Fluorescence quenching mechanism mainly includes static quenching or dynamic quenching, that is, the formation of complexes or collision inactivation [60]. In order to elucidate the quenching mechanism of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) complex by Cu2+, the Stern-Volmer plot was fitted (in Fig. 3 a) between the fluorescent intensity ratio F0/F and the concentration of Cu2+ to verify whether the dynamic quenching occurs [61]. The Stern-Volmer equation:

Where, F0 and F are the fluorescence emission intensities of the complex Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) before and after the addition of Cu2+, respectively, and KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. The Stern-Volmer plot demonstrates that it is not the conventional Stern Volmer equation, but an upward curvature [62]. This illustrates that the fluorescence quenching of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) complex by Cu2+ is not simple dynamic quenching, but exists synchronously in static and dynamic quenching [45,63].

The measurement of fluorescence lifetime is the best way to distinguish dynamic quenching from static quenching [62,64]. The fluorescence lifetime of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) before and after the addition of Cu2+ was measured by time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) experiments, as shown in Fig. 3b. All experimental fluorescence decay curves are fitted to a single exponential equation:
(3)

Where the offset values (A), amplitudes (B) and decay times (τ1) were extracted from non-linear fits with the function [34]. When 10 μM Cu2+ was added, the fluorescence lifetime of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) solution decreased from 0.5516 ms (τ0) to 0.4583 ms (τ), indicating that the quenching process of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) by Cu2+ contains dynamic quenching. And because τ0/τ = 1.20 is lower than F0/F = 2.73, indicating that the interaction between the excited state of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) and Cu2+ contains the static quenching [42]. Based on the reference on the Cu(II)-Eu(III) interaction [64], the relationship between UV–vis absorption spectra of Cu2+ containing samples and the emission spectrum of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) were also studied, as shown in Fig.S4. It can be seen from the figures that there is no overlapping area between the emission fluorescence spectra of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) and the UV–visible absorption spectra of Cu2+ and Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC). Therefore, it can be determined that the fluorescence quenching mechanism is not caused by Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) from Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) to Cu2+ or Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC). In conclusion, the quenching mechanism of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) by Cu2+ is a concomitant process of dynamic and static quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of Cys/Hcy/GSH test parameters by the Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: Because the sulfhydryl group of biothiols has strong complexation ability with Cu2+ to form Cu2+-S band [9,45], it is speculated that it will cause Cu2+ to fall off from Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC) complex, and then Eu3+ will be re-complexed with DTPA-bis(AMC). AMC as an antenna could sensitize Eu3+ once again, and the hypersensitive transition fluorescence at 5D0→7D2 (at 618 nm) will be restored to a certain extent. Therefore, the fluorescence response of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ to biothiols was studied. As shown in Fig. S5, Eu3+-DTPA-bis (AMC) exhibits pink fluorescence under excitation at 330 nm. When copper ions are added, the fluorescence is mainly the blue fluorescence of the AMC group. Fig. 4 a shown that, after adding a certain amount of biothiols (Cys/Hcy/GSH) to the solution, the fluorescence of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system at 618 nm was largely recovered. This is consistent with the above assumption.

Then, the fluorescence responses of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ system to other primary amino acids coexisting in the biological system were studied to investigate the specificity of Eu-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ to biothiols. Moreover, the fluorescence recovery efficiency Effr = (Fr-Fq)/(F0-Fq) [65], where F0 was the original fluorescence intensity of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) at 618 nm, Fr was the recovered fluorescence intensity of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ at 618 nm after adding the biothiols, and Fq was the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) at 618 nm after the addition of Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 4b, the quenched fluorescence intensity can be restored only after the biothiols are added to Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+. Other amino acids have little effect on the fluorescence intensity of the system. This result shows that Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ has excellent specificity for biothiols. Therefore, this turn-on mode could give specific detection on biothiols.

Before quantitative analysis of biothiols, the experimental conditions in Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system was optimized in detail to improve the sensitivity of the method, including the incubation time, the amount of Cu2+, and the concentration of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+. As illustrated in Fig. S6, after the addition of biothiols, the fluorescence recovery time had an effect on the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+. With the extension of reaction time, the fluorescence intensities at 618 nm based on the Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system increased gradually. When it reached 5.5 min, the fluorescence recovery intensities of the biothiols could achieve the maximum. Even if the time was prolonged, the fluorescence recovery intensities remain unchanged. This indicates that equilibrium states were reached between Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ and biothiols (Cys/Hcy/GSH). Therefore, it was appropriate to select the fluorescence recovery time of 5.5 min for the detection of biothiols in the sensing system of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+.

The concentration of Cu2+ in Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system not only affects the fluorescence quenching of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC), but also have a close relationship with the fluorescence recovery intensity. Therefore, the effect of the concentration ratio of biothiols (Cys/Hcy/GSH) to Cu2+ on the fluorescence recovery intensity at different Cu2+ concentrations was studied, as demonstrated in Fig. 5 . Take Cys as an example, a range of different concentrations (5, 10, 20 μM) Cu2+ were added to 10 μM Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) solution with the following addition of different concentrations of Cys according to corresponding proportions of [Cys]/[Cu2+]. It can be observed from Fig. 5a that the more Cu2+ were added, the greater the fluorescence quenching efficiency, but the more biothiols were needed to restore the fluorescence of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) system, which is not conducive to obtaining a lower detection limit. The less the concentration of Cu2+, the worse the quenching effect of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC). Considering that Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ as “off-on” fluorescence responds to biothiols, the most ideal concentration of Cu2+ was set as 10 μM to achieve a sensitive response to fluorescence quenching efficiency and fluorescence recovery intensity. A similar fluorescence response was observed in the experiments of Hcy and GSH (Fig. 5b-c). Therefore, 10 μM Cu2+ is the most reasonable concentration for the following study.

When Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) and Cu2+ were mixed into a sensing system with a concentration of 3.33 μM in the ratio of 1:1, an interesting phenomenon appeared in the experiment. That is, the addition of Cys significantly increased the fluorescence intensity of the Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system at 618 nm (Fig. 6 a). Meanwhile, the introduction of GSH and Hcy did not change the fluorescence signals at 618 nm. Under the condition that the concentration of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ is 3.33 μM, the selective and competitive experiments were carried out in the presence of Cys with other coexisting amino acids. As shown in Fig. 6b, the addition of other amino acids did not cause any interference to the detection of Cys, demonstrating that such a low concentration (3.33 μM) sensing system has very good selectivity and specificity for Cys over other coexisting amino acids (including GSH and Hcy). In view of the difference in selectivity to Cys/Hcy/GSH caused by the above Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ concentration, the fluorescence response of the sensing system with a series of concentrations to biothiols was studied, as shown in Fig. 6c. What interests us is the fact that the lower concentration (less than10 μM) sensing system increase the selectivity and specificity for Cys, while the higher concentration (≥10 μM) shows good selectivity for biothiols (Cys/Hcy/GSH). The reason for this phenomenon may be that as the concentration of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system decreased, the distance between Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ and the identified target increased, leading to the reduction in the probability of effective collision.. However, Cys had smaller steric hindrance and was easier to approach Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+, so its complexation ability to Cu2+ was stronger and more sensitive than Hcy and GSH [46,66]. As far as we know, the recognition ability of the sensing system to the target biothiols (Cys/Hcy/GSH) is related to its concentration, which is not reported by the researchers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determinations of Cys/Hcy/GSH by the Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence titration experiments of biothiols (Cys/Hcy/GSH) on Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system were studied, as shown in Fig. 7
a-c. The fluorescence intensity of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system increased gradually with the adding of biothiols (Cys/Hcy/GSH). Due to the strong affinity between Cu2+ and biothiols, Cu2+ falls off from Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC) system to form a non-fluorescent Cu-biothiols complex with Cu-S bonds. Meanwhile, a fluorescent Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) with the Eu3+ characteristic emission peak at 618 nm gradually formed. In most reported articles [23,24], the biothiols fluorescence sensors were designed according to the nucleophilic nature of sulfhydryl (-SH), which are often used to improve the nucleophilic activity in alkaline media. However, it is well known that biothiols are easily oxidized. The higher the pH value of the buffer solution, the easier they were oxidized. It can be seen that the acidic medium is conducive to effectively reduce the oxidation of biothiols and improves the detection accuracy. The pH 4.5 used in this experiment is more helpful to improve the accuracy and sensitivity of the sensing system. The fluorescence intensity at 618 nm of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system shows a good linear relationship with the concentration range of Cys (2.5–22.5 μM), Hcy (5–45 μM) and GSH (5–50 μM) (Fig. 7a-c inset), the regression curves are y = 24.052x + 370.82778 (R2 = 0.99212), y = 11.66743x + 336.65083 (R2 = 0.98881), y = 8.14871x + 403.00253 (R2 = 99814), and the corresponding limit of detection are 0.11 μM, 0.07 μM, 0.05 μM according to the formula LOD = 3σ/K, respectively.

In addition, based on the low concentration (3.33 μM) of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system, a series of studies about the incubation time, linear range, and LODs for Cys were further carried out in the Fig. S7 and Fig. 7d. The good linear range was obtained from 5 to 25 μM for Cys (R2 = 0.99148), and the LOD was calculated to be as low as 0.09 μM. These results are comparable or even superior to the previously reported sensing system of detection about biothiols (Table S1) [67,68], indicating that Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ as a new method has the potential to sensitively detect biothiols in biological systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing mechanism Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) for Cu2+ and Cys/Hcy/GSH in sequence
ce:section.ce:para: The sequential sensing mechanism of Cu2+ and Cys/Hcy/GSH by Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) as a multi-mode sensor is shown in Scheme 2 . In this scheme, the organic chromophore 7-amino-4-methyl coumarin (AMC) not only acts as an antenna for absorbing excitation light, but also as an energy donor, which transfers the absorbed energy to Eu3+ through the chelating agent diethyltriamine pentaacetic acid (DTPA). Therefore, Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) could be used as a fluorescent probe to emit the characteristic red fluorescence of europium (λem = 618 nm). According to the literature [42,57], DTPA-bis(AMC) has a higher binding constant for copper than europium at pH = 4.5. Therefore, Eu3+ in the complex could be replaced by Cu2+ to form Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC) (pH 4.5), resulting in fluorescence quenching of the emission peak at 618 nm. When biothiols are added to Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system, Cu2+ falls off from the complex Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC) due to its strong affinity with the sulfhydryl group of biothiols to form Cu2+-Cys/Hcy/GSH complex [46,69]. Eu3+ coordinates with chelator DTPA-bis(AMC) again to form the red fluorescent substance Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC), resulting in fluorescence recovery at 618 nm. Therefore, the complex Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) presents an “on-off-on” sensing mode for Cu2+ and biothiols in sequence.

High-resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS), fluorescence lifetime, and UV–vis absorption spectrometry were implemented to explain this detection mechanism. As shown in Fig. 8a, the peak of [Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC)] at m/z 767.14999 appeared clearly upon addition of Cu2+ to Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) system, which was consistent with the peaks generated by Cu2+ and DTPA-bis(AMC), and the signal belonging to [Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)] at m/z 856.13425 became very small. This significantly confirmed that Cu2+ could replace Eu3+ in the complex Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) to produce Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC). When Cys (take Cys as an example of biothiols) were added to the Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system, the strong peak corresponding to [Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)] reappeared at m/z 856.13435, and the signal of [Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC)] still exist at m/z 767.15036 but for the difference in m/z intensity merely, indicating that there was a dynamic balance between Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC), Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) and Cu2+-Cys. This is mainly due to the strong complexing affinity between Cu2+ and Cys, which separates a large amount of Cu2+ from Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC), and the free Eu3+ recombines with DTPA-bis(AMC), emitting the characteristic red fluorescence of Eu3+ at 618 nm. Since a small part of Cu2+ occupies DTPA-bis(AMC), the fluorescence intensity of the sensing system was not completely restored. Moreover, it can be seen from Fig. 4a that when biothiols were added to the Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system, the fluorescence recovery intensity was lower than that of the original Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC). This proves the existence of a competitive balance mechanism.

In addition, the sensing mechanism was further confirmed by the UV–vis absorption spectra of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) in the presence and absence of Cu2+, Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) + Cu2+ after the addition of biothiols (Cys/Hcy/GSH), and Cu2+ containing samples (Cys/Hcy/GSH) as shown in Fig. 8b. The absorption spectrum of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ was very similar to that of Cu2+ directly complexed with DTPA-bis(AMC), which confirmed that Cu2+ had a displacement reaction with Eu3+ in the complex Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC). In the presence of biothiols, the position and shape of the absorption peaks of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ system were basically consistent with that of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC), indicating the regeneration of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) complex. The absorption spectra of Cu2+ containing biothiols have no absorption peak at the corresponding position, so the change of the absorption peak of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) + Cu2+ after adding biothiols is not caused by the Cu2+-Cys/Hcy/GSH, which further proves the above reaction mechanism.

Furthermore, Fig. S8 shows the fluorescence decay curves of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) and Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ in the presence or absence of biothiols (Cys/Hcy/GSH) using a time-correlated single photon counter (TCSPC). The decay model used to fit the decay curves is single-exponential decay model according to equation (3). The lifetime, estimated errors and χ2 for each sample are listed in Table S2. The χ2 values for each result is close to 1. χ2 indicates the goodness of the fit between the single-exponential decay model and the experimental data. [64] The fluorescence lifetime of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) was calculated to be 0.5585 ms, which decreases to 0.4583 ms after adding Cu2+. The difference can be attributed to the complex substitution of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) with Cu2+ occurs, which is collisional inactivation [62] to generate a non-fluorescent complex Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC) that cause dynamic quenching or quenching mechanism of dynamic and static combination. When biothiols (Cys/Hcy/GSH) were added to Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ system, the fluorescence lifetimes of the system increased to 0.5345/0.5190/0.5044 ms, respectively. The recovery of fluorescence lifetime further shows that biothiols can displace Cu2+ by Cu2+-S bond to reform a red fluorescent complex Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and Cys in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the feasibility of the application of the sensing system in actual samples, the contents of Cu2+ in real water samples were determined by standard addition method based on Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) system, and the concentrations of Cys in fetal bovine serum (FBS), urine, milk, beverage and fresh juice were measured based on Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system.

As listed in Table 1 and Table S3, the calculated contents of Cu2+ and Cys were consistent with the amount of added samples. The recoveries of all spiked samples were calculated in the range of 96.20–106.80 %, and the RSD is less than 8.23 %. These results confirm that the sensing system is accurate and reliable in detecting Cu2+ in actual water samples and Cys in real biological samples, and has a good application prospect in the field of environment, biology and food.

In summary, a novel complex Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) with big Stokes shift (288 nm) was designed and synthesized using “on-off-on” fluorescence mode for highly sensitive and selective sequential recognition of Cu2+ and biothiols based on the displacement approach. In this system, AMC is used as an antenna to receive energy, and then transfer the absorbed energy to Eu3+, emitting the unique red fluorescence of lanthanide ions at 618 nm (5D0 → 7F2). With the addition of Cu2+, the red fluorescence at 618 nm of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) was obviously quenched due to the removement substitution reaction of Eu3+ from Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) by Cu2+ and the formation of non-fluorescent complex Cu2+-DTPA-bis(AMC). It has been successfully applied to the detection of Cu2+ in practical environmental water samples. When biothiols are introduced to Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC)/Cu2+ sensing system, the fluorescence of Eu3+-DTPA-bis(AMC) at 618 nm is restored due to the removal of Cu2+ and the formation of Cu-S bond complex. The limits of detection for Cu2+ and Cys/Hcy/GSH were 0.065 μM and 0.11/0.07/0.05 μM, respectively. This method provides some meaningful perspectives for the multifunctional detection of analytes existing in the field of environment, biology and food.
